evals.prompt: Assess sustainability for our workload: 20 m5.xlarge EC2 instances running 24/7 (avg 15% CPU), 100TB in S3 Standard (60% not accessed in 12 months), dev/staging environments running 24/7 identical to prod, all in us-east-1. No lifecycle policies anywhere.
evals.expected_output: A sustainability assessment identifying massive over-provisioning, storage waste, and unnecessary always-on environments as key improvement areas.
assertions: Identifies 15% CPU utilization as severe over-provisioning
Recommends right-sizing or Graviton migration for better perf/watt
Identifies 60% cold S3 data as waste and recommends lifecycle tiering (Glacier/Deep Archive)
Recommends scheduling dev/staging environments off during nights/weekends
Provides a sustainability scorecard with domain scores
Includes time-boxed remediation phases (Quick Wins, Foundation, Strategic)
References AWS Customer Carbon Footprint Tool

evals.prompt: sustainability review: serverless architecture using Lambda (arm64/Graviton), DynamoDB on-demand, S3 with Intelligent-Tiering, CloudFront, no EC2 instances. Already using scale-to-zero everywhere. What else can we improve?
evals.expected_output: An assessment that acknowledges the architecture is already sustainability-optimized and focuses on advanced improvements — code efficiency, payload optimization, regional carbon intensity.
assertions: Acknowledges the architecture is already well-optimized for sustainability
Does NOT recommend basic improvements already in place (Graviton, scale-to-zero, serverless)
Suggests advanced improvements (code efficiency, payload compression, caching to reduce invocations)
Mentions regional carbon intensity as a factor for workload placement
Identifies client-side impact opportunities (page weight, fewer API calls)

evals.prompt: We run a data pipeline processing 10TB daily: Kafka on EC2 (6 i3.2xlarge), Spark on EMR (20 m5.4xlarge for 8 hours/day), results to Redshift. Kafka runs 24/7 but peak throughput is only 4 hours/day. What's our sustainability posture?
evals.expected_output: A data pipeline-specific sustainability assessment recommending managed services, scheduling optimization, and right-sizing for the burst pattern.
assertions: Identifies Kafka running 24/7 for 4h peak as wasteful
Recommends Amazon MSK Serverless or scaling Kafka capacity to match demand
Recommends Graviton instances for EMR or suggests EMR Serverless
Addresses the 8h/day EMR cluster (already somewhat right-sized, but could use Spot)
Recommends S3 as intermediate storage instead of Kafka retention for cold data
Provides specific resource-hours or percentage waste estimates
